nutrient_source.title: Pork Stomach And Chicken Hot Pot 猪肚鸡小火锅 (包含一份米饭) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database, record cr_id G00010502
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database canonical entry for this dish (cr_id G00010502, confidence 0.95). The per_100g values reflect the hot pot component as recorded by HPB. The HPB entry reports 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g, indicating the nutritional data captures the protein-broth hot pot component; the rice portion is named in the dish title but its carbohydrate contribution is not reflected in the HPB per_100g figures. All macro and micronutrient values are used as-is from this approved primary regional source.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: The HPB per_100g data for this dish records 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g, reflecting the hot pot protein-broth component. Because the dish as a set meal includes a rice portion (not captured in the HPB per_100g figures), a GI of 40 is assigned as an analogous estimate based on the mixed protein-dominant hot pot component, consistent with low-GI values reported for high-protein meat-based dishes in Atkinson et al. (2008). Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 based on the HPB-recorded 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g. In practice, the included rice portion would raise the effective GL; however, the nutritional record as published by HPB does not capture the rice carbohydrate contribution in the per_100g values. GI category is set to 'low' reflecting the protein-dominant composition of the recorded component.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100 g — critically elevated, far exceeding the 600 mg/100 g clinical threshold
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid this dish; a single 500 g serving delivers approximately 5250 mg sodium, more than twice the 2300 mg daily recommended limit, posing a serious risk of acute blood pressure elevation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High protein (19.1 g/100 g) supports glycaemic stability, but the included rice portion and high sodium add metabolic burden
type2_diabetes.guidance: The hot pot component is low in carbohydrate and high in protein, which supports glycaemic control; however, patients with type 2 diabetes should request a smaller rice portion or substitute with cauliflower rice, and monitor overall sodium intake given the critically high 1050 mg/100 g sodium content.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100 g and cholesterol 89 mg/100 g combined with pork stomach (organ meat high in saturated fat and arachidonic acid) present significant cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to critically high sodium content (1050 mg/100 g), elevated cholesterol (89 mg/100 g), and the presence of pork stomach, an organ meat associated with high saturated fat and dietary cholesterol burden.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork stomach (red meat / organ meat) is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies inflammatory pathways; sodium 1050 mg/100 g worsens RA-related tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish; pork stomach is a red meat organ high in arachidonic acid that amplifies joint inflammation, and the critically high sodium content (1050 mg/100 g) exacerbates RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Stomach and Chicken Hot Pot (猪肚鸡小火锅) is a popular Chinese-Singaporean hot pot dish featuring sliced pork stomach (pig's stomach / tripe) and chicken pieces simmered together in a richly seasoned broth typically flavoured with white pepper, ginger, and aromatics. The dish is served as an individual mini hot pot set accompanied by a portion of steamed white rice. The pork stomach contributes a firm, chewy texture with a mild offal flavour, while the chicken provides tender, juicy protein. The broth is deeply savoury and peppery, characteristic of the Cantonese and Teochew culinary tradition popular in Singapore. Nutritionally, the hot pot component as recorded by HPB Singapore is high in protein (19.1 g/100 g) and fat (10.5 g/100 g), with negligible carbohydrate (0 g/100 g), reflecting the meat-and-broth composition. Sodium is critically high at 1050 mg/100 g due to the seasoned broth, making this dish unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. Cholesterol is elevated at 89 mg/100 g, consistent with the organ meat content. The dish is not halal due to pork content. It is suitable as a lunch or dinner main course and is commonly found in Chinese zi char restaurants and food courts in Singapore.

The dish contains pork and is not halal. The included rice portion in the dish name indicates a set meal, but the HPB nutritional data captures the hot pot component per 100 g.
